subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Emission of electrons in photoelectric effect can be suppressed by applying a sufficiently negative electron potential to the photoemissive substance.
Reason (R) : A negative electric potential, which stops the emission of electrons from the surface of a photoemissive substance, varies linearly with frequency of incident radiation.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Emission of electrons in photoelectric effect can be suppressed by applying a sufficiently negative electron potential to the photoemissive substance.
Reason (R) : A negative electric potential, which stops the emission of electrons from the surface of a photoemissive substance, varies linearly with frequency of incident radiation.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both \((\mathbf{A})\) and \((\mathbf{R})\) are true and \((\mathbf{R})\) is the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
Both \((\mathbf{A})\) and \((\mathbf{R})\) are true but \((\mathbf{R})\) is not the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
solution:
Assertion (A) is true because a sufficiently negative potential can repel and stop the emitted electrons from leaving the surface.
Reason (R) is also true because the stopping potential \(V_{\text {stop }}\) varies linearly with the frequency \(\nu\) of the incident light \(\left(V_{\text {stop }}=\frac{h}{e} \nu-\frac{\phi}{e}\right)\).
However, (R) does not directly explain why a negative potential suppresses electron emission; it only shows how much potential is needed for different frequencies.
Therefore, both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

q:
Hydrogen atom from excited state comes to the ground by emitting a photon of wavelength λ. The value of principal quantum number n of the excited state will be :

( R: Rydberg constant)
question:
Hydrogen atom from excited state comes to the ground by emitting a photon of wavelength λ. The value of principal quantum number n of the excited state will be :

( R: Rydberg constant)
solution:
When an electron jumps from a nth orbit to ground state, using Rydberg formula,

1λ=R1n12-1n22

In this case, n1=1 and n2=n. Therefore,

⇒1λ=R112-1n2⇒n=λRλR-1

q: Assuming the validity of Bohr's atomic model for hydrogen like ions the radius of $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its ground state is given by $\frac{1}{X} a_0$, where $X=$ _______. (Where $\mathrm{a}_0$ is the first Bohr's radius.)
question: Assuming the validity of Bohr's atomic model for hydrogen like ions the radius of $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its ground state is given by $\frac{1}{X} a_0$, where $X=$ _______. (Where $\mathrm{a}_0$ is the first Bohr's radius.)
solution:
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{r}_0 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{z}} \& \mathrm{z}=3$ for $\mathrm{Li}^{+2}$ and $\mathrm{n}=1$
$\therefore \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{r}_0 \frac{1^2}{3}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_0}{3} \quad \therefore \mathrm{x}=3$

q: Nucleus a having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>17</mn></math> and equal number of protons and neutrons has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleon. Another nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>26</mn></math> nucleons and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleons. The difference of binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
question: Nucleus a having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>17</mn></math> and equal number of protons and neutrons has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleon. Another nucleus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>26</mn></math> nucleons and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MeV</mi></math> binding energy per nucleons. The difference of binding energy of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>MeV</mi></math>.
solution:
In A, Z=17 and have equal numbers of protons and neutrons i.e. nucleons=2×17=34

So, for A, 

Total binding energy BEA=1.2×34=40.8 MeV

Another nucleus B mass number =26

So, total binding energy of  B is BEB=1.8×26 MeV=46.8 MeV

Difference of BE=BEB-BEA=6 MeV

q:
Radius of a certain orbit of hydrogen atom is $8.48 Ã…$. If energy of electron in this orbit is $E / x$. then $x=$ _____
(Given $\mathrm{a}_0=0.529 Ã…, E=$ energy of electron in ground state).
question:
Radius of a certain orbit of hydrogen atom is $8.48 Ã…$. If energy of electron in this orbit is $E / x$. then $x=$ _____
(Given $\mathrm{a}_0=0.529 Ã…, E=$ energy of electron in ground state).
solution:
We know $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}=0.529 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{\mathrm{Z}} \Rightarrow 8.48=0.529 \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{1} \\ & \mathrm{n}^2=16 \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4 \end{aligned}$
We know $\mathrm{E} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}$ $\begin{aligned} & E_{n^{\text {th }}}=\frac{E}{16} \\ & x=16 \end{aligned}$

(Given h=4.25×10-15 eVs)
solution:
Let the higher state be n.

The number of wavelengths emitted is given by,

n(n-1)2=6⇒n2-n-12=0⇒n-4(n+3)=0⇒n=4

For hydrogen atom,
hν=13.611-116=13.6×1516eV⇒ν=Eh=13.6×15×1.6×10-1916×6.62×10-34=3×1015Hz

q: The electron of a hydrogen atom makes a transition from the $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ orbit to the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit. For large $\mathrm{n}$ the wavelength of the emitted radiation is proportional to
question: The electron of a hydrogen atom makes a transition from the $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ orbit to the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit. For large $\mathrm{n}$ the wavelength of the emitted radiation is proportional to
solution:
If $n_1=n$ and $n_2=n+1$ Maximum wavelength $\lambda_{\max }=\frac{n^2\left(n+1^2\right)}{(2 n+1 R)}$
Therefore, for large $\mathrm{n}, \lambda_{\max } \propto n^3$

subject: Physics
q: The smallest wavelength of Lyman series is 91 nm. The difference between the largest wavelengths of Paschen and Balmer series is nearly $\_\_\_\_$ nm .
question: The smallest wavelength of Lyman series is 91 nm. The difference between the largest wavelengths of Paschen and Balmer series is nearly $\_\_\_\_$ nm .
solution:
Smallest wavelength of lyman
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R\left(\dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{\infty^2}\right)$
$R = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1}{91} \text{ nm}^{-1}$
$\lambda_{max}$ for balmer series
$n_1 = 2 \rightarrow n_2 = 3$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_B} = R\left(\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{9}\right)$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_B} = \dfrac{1}{91}\left(\dfrac{5}{36}\right)$
$\lambda_B = \left(\dfrac{91 \times 36}{5}\right) = 655.2 \text{ nm}$
$\lambda_{max}$ paschen
$n_1 = 3 \rightarrow n_2 = 4$
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_p} = \dfrac{1}{91}\left(\dfrac{1}{3^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2}\right) = \dfrac{1}{91} \times \dfrac{7}{144}$
$\lambda_p = \left(\dfrac{91 \times 144}{7}\right) = 1872 \text{ nm}$
$\Delta\lambda = \lambda_P - \lambda_B = 1872 - 655.2$
$\Delta\lambda = 1216.8$
$\Delta\lambda \approx 1217$

subject: Physics
q:
In the hydrogen atom, the electron makes a transition from the higher orbit ($i$) to a lower orbit ($f$). The ratio of the radius of the orbits in given by $r_i : r_f = 16 : 4$. The wavelength of photon emitted due to this transition is _____ nm.
(Given Rydberg constant $= 1.0973 \times 10^7$ /m)
question:
In the hydrogen atom, the electron makes a transition from the higher orbit ($i$) to a lower orbit ($f$). The ratio of the radius of the orbits in given by $r_i : r_f = 16 : 4$. The wavelength of photon emitted due to this transition is _____ nm.
(Given Rydberg constant $= 1.0973 \times 10^7$ /m)
solution:
The radius of the $n$-th orbit in a hydrogen atom is proportional to the square of the principal quantum number $n$, i.e., $r_n \propto n^2$.

Given the ratio of the radii of the initial and final orbits:
$\dfrac{r_i}{r_f} = \dfrac{16}{4}$

This implies:
$\dfrac{n_i^2}{n_f^2} = \dfrac{16}{4} \Rightarrow n_i = 4 \text{ and } n_f = 2$

The wavelength $\lambda$ of the emitted photon is given by the Rydberg formula:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R \left( \dfrac{1}{n_f^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_i^2} \right)$

Substituting the values:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.0973 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{2^2} - \dfrac{1}{4^2} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.0973 \times 10^7 \left( \dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{16} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = 1.0973 \times 10^7 \times \dfrac{3}{16}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{16}{3 \times 1.0973 \times 10^7} \text{ m}$

$\lambda \approx 4.86 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 486 \text{ nm}$

Answer: $486$

subject: Physics
q: In the Bohr model an electron moves in a circular orbit around the proton. Considering the orbiting electron to be a circular current loop, the magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $n^{\text {th }}$ excited state, is :
question: In the Bohr model an electron moves in a circular orbit around the proton. Considering the orbiting electron to be a circular current loop, the magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $n^{\text {th }}$ excited state, is :
solution:
Magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom, when the electron is in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ excited state, i.e., $n^{\prime}=(n+1)$
As magnetic moment $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{n}}=\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{A}=\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{n}}\left(\pi \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}^2\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
& i_n=e V_n=\frac{m z^2 e^5}{4 \varepsilon_0^2 n^3 h^3} \\
& r_n=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 k z m e^2}\left(k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0}\right)
\end{aligned}$
Solving we get magnetic moment of the hydrogen atom for $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ excited state
$M_{n^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{e}{2 m}\right) \frac{n h}{2 \pi}$

solution:
v∝ z n

r∝ n 2 z

B= μI 2R

I= e T = e ( 2πR v ) = e 2π v R

B= μ 0 2 e 2π v R 2

B= μ 0 e 4π z n ( n 2 z ) 2

B=μ0e4πz3n5

B∝ n −5 .

(C) the energy of H atom in its ground state is same as that of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion for its ground state.
(D) the energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its ground state

question:
Considering Bohr's atomic model for hydrogen atom :
(A) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state.
(B) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its second excited state.
(C) the energy of H atom in its ground state is same as that of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion for its ground state.
(D) the energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its ground state
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E} \propto \frac{\mathrm{Z}}{\mathrm{n}^2} \\ & \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{H}}=1 \quad \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=2 \quad \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{Li}^{+2}}=3\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 1^{\text {st }} \text { excited state } \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2 \\ & 2^{\text {nd }} \text { excited state } \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3\end{aligned}$
From the given statements only A \& B are correct.

Δ E = h ν
ν = Δ E h = k 1 n - 1 2 - 1 n 2 = k 2 n n 2 n - 1 2

≈ 2 k n 3 ∝ 1 n 3

q: A light of energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The atom absorbs the radiation and reaches to one of its excited states. The angular momentum of the atom in the excited state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>17</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A light of energy <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> is incident on a hydrogen atom in its ground state. The atom absorbs the radiation and reaches to one of its excited states. The angular momentum of the atom in the excited state is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mi>π</mi></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>17</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>15</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>eVs</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Let the electron jumped to nth excited state.

In the ground state, energy E=-13.6 eV

So, using relation 12.75=13.6112-1n2

⇒0.9375=1-1n2⇒n2=16

⇒n=16=4

Now, angular momentum, L=nh2π=4h2π=2hπ

 =2π×4.14×10-15

=828×10-17π eVs

Hence, the value of x=828.

q: A neutron moving with a speed 'v' makes a head on collision with a stationary hydrogen atom in ground state. The minimum kinetic energy of the neutron for which  perfactly inelastic collision will take place is :
question: A neutron moving with a speed 'v' makes a head on collision with a stationary hydrogen atom in ground state. The minimum kinetic energy of the neutron for which  perfactly inelastic collision will take place is :
solution:
Let velocity before collision v from momentum conservation mv=( m+m ) v 1

v 1 = v 2

Loss in K.E. =12mv2-122mv22

=14 mv2

K.E. lost is used to jump from 1st orbit to 2nd orbit ∆ K.E.=10.2 eV

⇒  14 mv2=10.2

12 mv2=2×10.2=20.4 eV

subject: Physics
q: Choose the correct option from the following options given below :
question: Choose the correct option from the following options given below :
options: In the ground state of Rutherford's model electrons are in stable equilibrium. While in Thomson's model electrons always experience a net-force.
An atom has a nearly continuous mass distribution in a Rutherford's model but has a highly non-uniform mass distribution in Thomson's model
A classical atom based on Rutherford's model is doomed to collapse.
The positively charged part of the atom possesses most of the mass in Rutherford's model but not in Thomson's model.
solution:
According to Rutherford's model, most of the mass of the atom is concentrated at the centre (nucleus) and the electron revolves around it.

As the electron is moving in a circular motion, it is accelerated. According to Maxwell accelerated charged particle loses its energy in the form of electro-magnetic radiation.

Therefore, a classical atom based on Rutherford's model is doomed to collapse.

subject: Physics
q: In a hydrogen atom the electron makes a transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math>  level to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> level. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></math>, the frequency of radiation emitted is proportional to :
question: In a hydrogen atom the electron makes a transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math>  level to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> level. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></math>, the frequency of radiation emitted is proportional to :
solution:
En=-Rhcn2
En+1=-Rhc(n+1)2
ΔE=En+1-En
hv=Rhc1n2-1(n+1)2
v=R.cn+12-n2n2n+12
v=R.c1+2nn2n+12

n >> 1 ⇒v=R.c2nn2×n2=2RCn3

v∞1n3

subject: Physics
q: The energy levels of an hydrogen atom are shown below. The transition corresponding to emission of shortest wavelength is
question: The energy levels of an hydrogen atom are shown below. The transition corresponding to emission of shortest wavelength is
solution:
Wavelength of photons released during de-excitation is given by,

1λ=R1n12-1n22.

Clearly, shortest wavelength will correspond to highest energy difference in de-excitation, which in the given figure will correspond to transition from n=3 to n=1.

subject: Physics
q:
The angular momentum of an electron in a hydrogen atom is proportional to :
(Where $r$ is the radius of orbit of electron)
question:
The angular momentum of an electron in a hydrogen atom is proportional to :
(Where $r$ is the radius of orbit of electron)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{Kq}_1 \mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{r}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \mathrm{mv}^2 \mathrm{r}^2=K \mathrm{~K}_1 \mathrm{q}_2 \mathrm{r} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{L}^2}{\mathrm{~m}}=K \mathrm{Kq}_1 \mathrm{q}_2 \mathrm{r} \\ & \mathrm{L} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{r}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A hydrogen atom in its ground state absorbs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> of energy. The angular momentum of electron of the hydrogen atom will increase by the value of (Given, Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Js</mi></math>).
question: A hydrogen atom in its ground state absorbs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>eV</mi></math> of energy. The angular momentum of electron of the hydrogen atom will increase by the value of (Given, Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mi>Js</mi></math>).
solution:
Energy absorbed is given by,

ΔE=13.6×1n12-1n22

10.2=13.6×1n12-1n22

Therefore, we get, n1=1 & n2=2

The change in angular momentum

ΔL=n2h2π-n1h2π=h2π

=1.05×10-34 J s

subject: Physics
q: Considering the Bohr model of hydrogen like atoms, the ratio of the ratio of the radius $5^{\text {th }}$ orbit of the electron in $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is
question: Considering the Bohr model of hydrogen like atoms, the ratio of the ratio of the radius $5^{\text {th }}$ orbit of the electron in $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is
solution:
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{r} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{2}$
for $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$
$r_5=r \cdot \frac{25}{3}$
for $\mathrm{He}^{+}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{r}_5=\mathrm{r} \cdot \frac{25}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}}=\frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}$
